metadata.title: En Chakai — Tea ceremony in the side of Tokyo most travelers never see
metadata.description: A 60-minute tea ceremony in a Bunkyō tea room, hosted in English. From ¥7,000. Reservations confirmed by hand within 24 hours.

hero.heading:
Tea, in the side of Tokyo
most travelers never see.
hero.tagline: A sixty-minute ceremony in a quiet ward of north Tokyo. Hosted in English. No seiza required.

editorsNote.heading: We started this because the alternatives weren't quite right.
editorsNote.body: The tea ceremonies most visitors find in Tokyo are either too cheap to be the real thing or too expensive to be relaxed. This is the middle. Sixty minutes, four guests at a time, in a working tea room in Bunkyō — the part of Tokyo where a lot of people quietly live and very few tourists ever come. We thought you should know it exists.

neighborhoodsPreview.heading: Five small worlds, all within walking distance.
neighborhoodsPreview.body: We bundle the ceremony with a guide to the streets around it, because half the point of coming this way is that you didn't have to.

ceremony.heading: Sixty minutes, end to end.
ceremony.body: What actually happens, in order. No surprises, no improvisation theater.

timeline.label: A short introduction — what to watch for
Whisk your own bowl, with help

plans.heading: Choose the room, not the activity.
plans.body: All three are the same ceremony. The difference is privacy and pace.

encounter.name: The Encounter
encounter.tagline: The shortest version. Useful if you have an afternoon to fill.

conversation.name: The Conversation
conversation.tagline: Our standard hour. Most first-timers pick this.

quietHour.name: The Quiet Hour
quietHour.tagline: Same ceremony, your party only. We don't add another booking.

host.heading: Who you'll be sitting across from.
host.name: Tea Master Yamamoto
host.pull: There is no competition in tea, no hurry, no judgement. Only this single hour, between the two of us.
host.bio: Trained in Urasenke since youth, our host has welcomed guests from more than thirty countries. She speaks English fluently and treats first-timers and old hands the same way.

reserve.heading: We confirm by hand, within 24 hours.
reserve.body: Send us a request and we'll reply with availability and a payment link. No automated calendar — every booking is reviewed personally.
reserve.review1: The most thoughtful tea ceremony we found in Tokyo. Calm, unhurried, in a real neighborhood. — Sarah M., New York
reserve.review2: We were expecting a tourist demo. We got something quieter and much better. — James T., London
reserve.review3: The host explained as much or as little as we asked for. Pitch-perfect. — Emily R., Melbourne

experience.metaDescription: What happens in 60 minutes, what to expect, and how the three plans differ.
experience.heading: This is not a tourist demo.
experience.lede: The ceremony you'll attend is the one our host studied for twenty-five years. The only thing we changed for visitors is the language and the option to use a chair.

items.title: Chair seating available
items.body: Tatami if you want it, a low chair if you don't. Either is fine.

items.title: No kimono provided
items.body: Wear what you came in. Light socks help — guests sit on tatami, even if you take a chair.

items.title: Photography is welcome
items.body: Quietly, between movements. The host will tell you when.

items.title: Card only, in JPY
items.body: Visa, Master, Amex, JCB via Stripe. We send the link in the confirmation email.

neighborhoods.metaDescription: Five small worlds within walking distance of the tea room: Sengoku, Hakusan, Sugamo, Gokokuji, Zoshigaya.
neighborhoods.heading: Five small worlds within walking distance.
neighborhoods.lede: We host the ceremony in Bunkyō, a residential ward in north Tokyo that almost no guidebook covers. The five places below are all within a half-hour walk of the tea room. None of them feel like Tokyo as the brochure imagines it. That's the point.
neighborhoods.footer: Plan a route: see our suggested itineraries.
neighborhoods.walk: {n} min walk from the tea room

sengoku.lede: Sengoku is, by Tokyo standards, very quiet. The tea room sits on a side street that you would walk past without noticing.
paras: There are two stations called Sengoku. We mean the one on the Toei Mita line — five minutes' walk and you are at our door. The other one, on the Yamanote loop, is twenty minutes south and a different conversation entirely.

What you find here is not picturesque exactly. Old timber houses next to small concrete apartment blocks, a koban on the corner, an elderly man watering his hydrangeas. People live here. That is the appeal.

If you have an extra fifteen minutes before or after the ceremony, walk south to the small Tokugawa-era graveyard at Jōrenji, or north along Hakusan-dōri toward the shrine. Neither is a destination. Both are pleasant.

hakusan.lede: Eight minutes from the tea room, on a low hill, sits a shrine that was here before Tokyo was Tokyo.
paras: Hakusan Shrine has been on this hill since the 10th century, though the buildings you see today are mostly post-war. It is the kind of shrine the city walks past on its way to work — busy on festival days, otherwise yours.

Come in mid-June if you can: three thousand hydrangeas bloom across the hillside for the Bunkyō Hydrangea Festival, and the side path turns into a tunnel of blue and white. Tōyō University is next door, so the streets have the slow rhythm of a university quarter — bookshops, ramen, students reading on benches.
Hakusan station, on the Toei Mita line, is one stop from Sengoku. You can do the shrine before or after the ceremony without backtracking.

sugamo.lede: Sugamo is what locals affectionately call Harajuku for the elderly. We mean that as a compliment.
paras: The Jizō-dōri shopping street runs four hundred meters and is, on the 4th, 14th, and 24th of each month, the busiest place for two kilometers. Older women come for healing-salt at Togenuki Jizō, then move on to red underwear (a local good-luck specialty), then to lunch.

Eat where the queue is. The unagi shops are good. The shio-daifuku at Mizuno is the thing to bring back. The temple itself is small and unassuming — the point is the street it sits on, and the people on it.
From Sugamo to the tea room is twelve minutes on foot, mostly downhill. A nice route is: Jizō-dōri, then south through the back streets, then the ceremony, then a coffee.

gokokuji.lede: A 1681 temple commissioned by the Shogun's mother. One of the few large structures in central Tokyo to survive both the Great Kantō Earthquake and the bombings.
paras: Gokokuji is bigger than it looks from the gate, and almost always nearly empty. The main hall is original. The grounds slope back into a quiet temple cemetery, and if you wander in you will find — among many graves — those of three successive Urasenke tea masters.
We send guests here when they want to sit somewhere old without paying admission. The grounds are always open. Bring a coffee.
The temple is eighteen minutes' walk from the tea room, or one stop on the Yūrakuchō line from Gokokuji station to Edogawabashi and a slow walk back.

zoshigaya.lede: If you read Sōseki or Lafcadio Hearn, you already know this neighborhood, even if you don't know its name.
paras: Zoshigaya is where Tokyo, briefly, slows down. The Kishimojin temple is its center — a small, perfectly proportioned compound with a 700-year-old camphor tree and a candy shop that has been there for two hundred years.

The cemetery a few blocks away holds Natsume Sōseki, Lafcadio Hearn, John Manjirō, and a long list of writers and printers. It is large, walkable, and never crowded. There are signs in English at the major graves.

Take the Toden Arakawa tram from Kishimojin-mae if you can — it is one of two surviving streetcar lines in Tokyo, and the ride out toward Waseda is the kind of thing that makes a slow afternoon feel earned.

itineraries.metaDescription: Three suggested routes that combine the tea ceremony with the neighborhoods around it.
itineraries.heading: Three ways to spend a day around the tea room.
itineraries.lede: We get asked this a lot. These are the routes we actually recommend, in the order our guests usually do them.

booking.metaDescription: Send a reservation request. We confirm by hand within 24 hours and send a payment link.
booking.heading: Confirmed by hand, within 24 hours.
booking.lede: We host one party at a time. Every reservation is reviewed personally — usually within a few hours, always within a day.

items.title: Send a request
items.body: Five minutes on a short Google Form. Date, party size, plan, anything we should know.

items.title: We reply
items.body: Within 24 hours. If your date is open, we send a Stripe payment link. If not, we suggest the nearest available.

items.title: Pay to confirm
items.body: Card payment in JPY. Visa, Master, Amex, JCB. The seat is yours.

preview.heading: What we'll ask
fields: Experience plan (The Encounter ¥7,000 / The Conversation ¥10,000 / The Quiet Hour ¥12,000)

items.title: Card payment only
items.body: Visa, Master, Amex, JCB via Stripe. UK cards: 3D Secure may apply.

items.title: English-hosted
items.body: The ceremony, the explanation, the Q&A — all in English.

items.title: Chair seating available
items.body: If you can't or don't want to do seiza, say so on the form.

items.title: We don't lend kimono
items.body: Wear what you came in. Light socks help.

booking.ctaNote: Opens in a new tab. Replies usually within a few hours.

faq.metaDescription: Common questions about the tea ceremony, booking, payment, cancellation, and access.
faq.heading: Common questions, answered plainly.

items.a: Send a request via the Google Form. We reply within 24 hours with availability and a Stripe payment link. Pay to confirm.
We host one party at a time and want to be sure we can do the date well. The review is fast — usually a few hours.

items.q: How far in advance can I book?
items.a: Up to three months. Same-week requests are sometimes possible — ask.
Visa, Master, Amex, JCB. We use Stripe Payment Links, billed in JPY.

items.q: Will my UK or US bank charge a fee?
items.a: Most cards charge a small foreign-transaction fee on JPY. We can't waive that. The amount your bank shows in your local currency may differ slightly from the JPY total because of the exchange rate.
It's the simplest for us and the most accurate for you — your bank applies its own exchange rate, which is usually better than ours would be.

items.q: Do I have to do seiza?
items.a: No. We have low chairs. Tell us on the form so we set the room up correctly.
Anything comfortable. Light or white socks are traditional and help with the tatami. We don't lend kimono.
Quietly, between movements. The host will tell you when.
Forty-five, sixty, or seventy-five minutes depending on the plan. Allow another fifteen minutes either side.
7 or more days before: full refund. 3–6 days before: 50%. 2 days or less: no refund. We process refunds via the original Stripe charge.
Full refund and an apology. Rare, but it does happen — typhoons, illness.
Sengoku, Bunkyō-ku, Tokyo. Five minutes' walk from Sengoku station on the Toei Mita line. The exact address is sent with the booking confirmation.
There are two small steps at the entrance. Tell us on the form if mobility is a concern and we'll make it work.

footer.tagline: A tea ceremony in the part of Tokyo people quietly live in.
